Vertical Farms and Urban Food Security Nexus
============================================

By[Sustainability Directory](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/author/sustainability_directory/ "Posts by Sustainability Directory")_7 March 2025_[No Comments](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/vertical-farms-and-urban-food-security-nexus/#respond)26 min

![Image 4: This arrangement presents a futuristic interface within Prism Predicted Sustainable Future Concept, with Possible Scenarios and Outcomes for Future and Tech in AI, Sustainablity, etc, with careful juxtaposition of angular surfaces, offering reflective capabilities with resource utilization efficiency. Its composition highlights technological integration with potential in environmentally-conscious architecture. The integration of sustainability and design points to future building material breakthroughs promoting carbon neutrality and contributing to positive societal environmental stewardship.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/innovative-sustainable-design-aspects-in-advanced-materials-a-study-of.jpg)

Assess
------

The nexus of **vertical farms and urban food security** represents a significant area of innovation within the broader context of sustainable futures. As global populations increasingly concentrate in urban centers, the challenge of ensuring consistent, reliable access to nutritious food intensifies. Traditional agricultural models, often located far from urban areas, face mounting pressures from land scarcity, water limitations, [climate change Meaning → Climate Change represents the principal global challenge of the 21st century, defining the strategic, economic, and ethical frameworks for sustainable development.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/climate-change/) impacts, and complex supply chains.

These factors collectively underscore the vulnerability of conventional [food systems Meaning → Food Systems represent the complex web of activities and actors involved in producing, processing, distributing, consuming, and disposing of food.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-systems/) in meeting the demands of burgeoning city populations. Vertical farming, an advanced form of controlled environment agriculture, presents itself as a technologically driven approach to mitigate these vulnerabilities, promising to reshape urban food landscapes.

> Vertical farms offer a potential pathway to enhance urban food security by localizing production and optimizing resource use.

Vertical farms, characterized by their multi-layered structures within controlled indoor environments, optimize space utilization, a critical advantage in densely populated urban settings. Unlike conventional agriculture, these systems drastically reduce water consumption through recirculating hydroponic or aeroponic systems, addressing water scarcity concerns prevalent in many regions. Furthermore, the controlled environment minimizes the need for pesticides and herbicides, leading to potentially healthier and safer produce. Proximity to consumers translates to reduced transportation distances, lowering carbon emissions associated with food distribution and decreasing post-harvest losses.

These inherent efficiencies position vertical farms as a compelling component of future urban food systems. However, the current landscape of vertical farming is far from universally adopted or seamlessly integrated into mainstream food production. Understanding the current baseline requires a critical examination of the technological, economic, social, and environmental dimensions of this evolving sector.

![Image 5: This close up details futuristic arrangement of nanofibers at a central locus presenting innovations in sustainable material design. It reveals a nexus point for precision manufacturing using green technology that allows for eco friendly compositing. It exemplifies innovative development within material research and the possibility of artificial intelligence integration towards environmentally sound production using AI and next generation nanofiber creation leading towards a circular economy.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-material-innovation-future-focused-nanofiber-structures-high.jpg)
### Current State of Vertical Farming

Technologically, vertical farming is characterized by rapid advancements in LED lighting, environmental control systems, automation, and sensor technologies. **LED lighting** has become increasingly energy-efficient and spectrally tunable, allowing for optimized plant growth and resource utilization. Sophisticated climate control systems regulate temperature, humidity, and CO2 levels within the growing environment, creating ideal conditions year-round, irrespective of external climate fluctuations. Automation, ranging from automated planting and harvesting to environmental monitoring, aims to reduce labor costs and improve operational efficiency.

Data analytics and sensor technologies are increasingly employed to monitor plant health, nutrient levels, and environmental parameters, enabling data-driven optimization of growing protocols. These technological advancements have significantly improved the productivity and viability of vertical farms in recent years.

Economically, [vertical farming Meaning → Vertical Farming represents a significant strategic response to escalating global food system vulnerabilities, resource scarcity, and the increasing pressures of urbanization.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/vertical-farming/) faces significant hurdles. High initial capital costs associated with infrastructure development, including specialized buildings, lighting systems, and environmental controls, represent a major barrier to entry. Operational expenses, particularly energy consumption for lighting and climate control, can be substantial, impacting profitability. Labor costs, while potentially reduced through automation, remain a factor, especially in regions with higher wages.

Market access and competition with established agricultural supply chains also present challenges. The [economic viability Meaning → Economic Viability signifies the capacity of an enterprise, project, or policy to sustain its operations and achieve its objectives over the long term without compromising the ecological systems or social structures upon which it depends.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/economic-viability/) of vertical farms is highly dependent on factors such as crop selection, energy costs, technological efficiency, and market demand for locally and sustainably produced food. Current economic models suggest that vertical farming is most competitive for high-value crops, specialty produce, and regions with high import costs or limited access to fresh produce.

Socially, vertical farming holds the promise of enhancing urban food security, creating local employment opportunities, and promoting [community engagement Meaning → Community Engagement represents the structured and intentional process of building reciprocal relationships between entities—such as corporations, governments, or non-governmental organizations—and the populations or communities with which they interact.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/community-engagement/) in food production. By locating food production within cities, vertical farms can improve access to fresh, nutritious food, particularly in underserved urban communities and food deserts. They can also serve as educational platforms, raising [awareness about sustainable agriculture Meaning → Understanding the broad impacts of buying choices, from resource use to social justice, to encourage responsible consumption.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/sustainable-consumption-awareness/) and food systems. The creation of urban farming jobs can contribute to local economic development and provide opportunities for skill development in emerging agricultural technologies.

However, the social impact of vertical farms is also contingent on [equitable access Meaning → Equitable Access signifies the fair distribution of environmental benefits and burdens, ensuring that all individuals and communities, irrespective of socio-economic status, geography, or background, have the opportunity to benefit from and contribute to sustainable development.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/equitable-access/) to their products and benefits. Ensuring affordability and accessibility of vertical farm produce for all segments of the urban population is crucial for realizing their potential to address food insecurity. Furthermore, community integration and acceptance of vertical farming as a legitimate and valuable part of the urban [food system Meaning → The Food System constitutes a fundamental nexus for global sustainability, representing the aggregate of all processes and infrastructures involved in feeding populations, from agricultural production and resource management to distribution, consumption, and waste management.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-system/) are important social factors to consider.

Environmentally, vertical farms present a mixed picture. While they offer significant advantages in terms of water conservation, reduced pesticide use, and minimized [transportation Meaning → Transportation represents the global infrastructure and operational systems that facilitate the movement of people and goods, underpinning economic activity, social connectivity, and resource distribution.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/transportation/) emissions, their energy consumption is a major environmental concern. The reliance on artificial lighting, especially if powered by fossil fuels, can result in a substantial carbon footprint. Life cycle assessments of vertical farms often highlight energy consumption as a critical factor influencing their overall environmental impact.

However, advancements in [renewable energy Meaning → The concept of 'Renewable Energy' signifies a fundamental shift in global energy systems, representing a strategic imperative for climate change mitigation and sustainable development.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/renewable-energy/) integration, such as solar and wind power, offer pathways to mitigate this concern and enhance the [environmental sustainability Meaning → Environmental Sustainability signifies the fundamental objective of maintaining the planet's ecological systems and resource bases to support life and human societies indefinitely.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/environmental-sustainability/) of vertical farms. Furthermore, waste management and resource recycling within vertical farming systems are areas requiring ongoing attention to minimize environmental burdens and promote circular economy principles. The environmental performance of vertical farms is thus highly dependent on energy sources, resource management practices, and technological optimization.

![Image 6: This sustainable dark concrete facade is designed with circular spheres for dimensional depth enhancing modern architectural vision with an environmental angle. Light plays across the textured surface, hinting at technology’s role and the balance needed within urban spaces by incorporating AI. It demonstrates thoughtful construction techniques aligning with sustainable development goals promoting environmental resilience.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-concrete-facade-with-circular-elements-reflecting-predicted-urban.jpg)
### Data Patterns and Predictive Analysis

Analyzing current data patterns reveals both encouraging trends and persistent challenges for the vertical farming sector. Market research indicates a **growing global market for vertical farming**, driven by increasing urbanization, [food security Meaning → Food Security represents a foundational pillar for societal stability, human well-being, and planetary health, situated within broader discourse on sustainable resource governance, equitable access, and resilience.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-security/) concerns, and consumer demand for sustainable and locally sourced produce. Investments in vertical farming technology and infrastructure are on the rise, signaling growing investor confidence in the sector’s potential. Technological advancements continue to drive improvements in energy efficiency, crop yields, and automation capabilities, enhancing the operational viability of vertical farms.

Consumer awareness and acceptance of vertical farm produce are also increasing, as consumers become more conscious of food origins and sustainability attributes. These positive data patterns suggest a trajectory of growth and increasing adoption for vertical farming in urban environments.

> Data indicates a growing market and technological advancements in vertical farming, yet economic viability and environmental impact remain critical considerations for widespread adoption.

However, data also highlight persistent challenges. **Economic viability** remains a key concern, with many vertical farms struggling to achieve profitability, particularly in competitive food markets or regions with high energy costs. Energy consumption continues to be a significant operational expense and environmental burden, limiting the overall sustainability of some vertical farming operations. Social equity and access issues persist, with vertical farm produce often positioned as premium products, potentially excluding lower-income urban populations.

Regulatory frameworks and policy support for vertical farming are still evolving in many regions, creating uncertainty and barriers to development. These challenges indicate that widespread and equitable adoption of vertical farming requires concerted efforts to address economic viability, environmental sustainability, and social accessibility.

Predictive analysis, based on current data trends and technological trajectories, suggests several potential future scenarios for the [vertical farms and urban food security Vertical farms: cultivating cityscapes, securing future food.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/vertical-farms-and-urban-food-security/) nexus. Positive scenarios envision breakthroughs in renewable energy integration, significantly reducing the [carbon footprint Meaning → It signifies the aggregated greenhouse gas emissions attributable to an individual, organization, event, or product, serving as a critical metric within global climate discourse and strategic sustainability planning.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/carbon-footprint/) of vertical farms and enhancing their environmental sustainability. Technological advancements in automation and AI could drive down operational costs and improve efficiency, making vertical farm produce more economically competitive. Policy support, including incentives for urban agriculture and sustainable food systems, could create a more favorable regulatory and economic environment for vertical farming development.

Social innovations, such as community-owned vertical farms and [urban farming Meaning → Urban Farming represents a strategic shift towards localized, resilient food systems within metropolitan areas.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/urban-farming/) cooperatives, could enhance [social equity Meaning → Social Equity represents a fundamental dimension of sustainable development, signifying the imperative for fair distribution of environmental benefits and burdens, and equitable access to resources, opportunities, and decision-making processes across all societal strata.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/social-equity/) and access to vertical farm benefits. These positive predictions point towards a future where vertical farms play a significant role in enhancing urban food security and promoting sustainable urban development.

Conversely, negative scenarios highlight potential risks and limitations. If energy costs remain high or [renewable energy adoption Behavioral economics is key to unlocking widespread renewable energy adoption by understanding psychological influences on decision-making.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/scenario/behavioral-economics-impact-on-renewable-energy-adoption/) lags, the environmental footprint of vertical farms could undermine their sustainability claims. Economic challenges could persist, limiting the scalability and widespread adoption of vertical farming, particularly if technological advancements fail to deliver anticipated cost reductions. Social inequalities could be exacerbated if vertical farm produce remains inaccessible to low-income populations, widening the gap in food security.

Unforeseen technological disruptions or market shifts could also impact the viability of vertical farming investments. These negative predictions underscore the importance of proactive measures to address potential challenges and ensure that vertical farming realizes its full potential to contribute to [urban food security Meaning → Urban Food Security represents a critical dimension of sustainable urban development, situating food systems as integral to metropolitan resilience, public health, and social equity.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/urban-food-security/) and sustainability.

![Image 7: Monochrome architectural study features nested portals gazing at a star speckled sky, indicative of sustainable futuristic design for the built environment. The smooth sustainable tech surfaces suggest environmentally conscious material science fostering green solutions. Integrated architecture symbolizes AI driven technology, aiming to provide futuristic landscapes with an eco friendly core focused on sustainability and long lasting strategies supporting the earth's environmental equilibrium.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/exploring-sustainable-futures-a-minimalist-rendering-of-an-eco-tech-vision.jpg)
### Cross-Dimensional Influences

The development and impact of vertical farms are influenced by a complex interplay of cross-dimensional factors. **Cross-cultural** perspectives shape consumer acceptance of vertical farm produce, with varying cultural preferences for food origins, production methods, and sustainability attributes. In some cultures, locally produced food and innovative agricultural technologies may be highly valued, while others may prioritize traditional farming practices or be skeptical of indoor-grown produce. Understanding and addressing these cultural nuances is crucial for successful market penetration and social integration of vertical farming in diverse urban contexts.

**Cross-social** dynamics influence access to vertical farm benefits and the distribution of their impacts. Social inequalities, income disparities, and food access disparities within urban populations can shape who benefits from vertical farm produce and employment opportunities. Ensuring equitable access and addressing potential social divides requires careful consideration of pricing strategies, distribution networks, and community engagement initiatives. Furthermore, social perceptions of vertical farming as a futuristic or niche technology versus a mainstream food production system can influence public support and policy adoption.

**Cross-environmental** factors are intrinsically linked to the sustainability of vertical farms. Climate change impacts, such as increasing frequency of extreme weather events and water scarcity, can both create opportunities for and pose challenges to vertical farming. Controlled environments offer resilience to climate variability, but energy consumption and resource management practices must be optimized to minimize environmental burdens. The broader environmental context of urban ecosystems, including air quality, biodiversity, and urban heat island effects, can also interact with vertical farming operations, requiring holistic and integrated approaches to urban sustainability.

**Cross-sectorial** influences, encompassing policy, technology, economics, and social sectors, shape the enabling environment for vertical farming development. Policy frameworks related to urban planning, agriculture, energy, and sustainability can either facilitate or hinder the growth of vertical farming. Technological advancements in related sectors, such as renewable energy, automation, and materials science, can drive innovation and efficiency in vertical farming. Economic factors, such as energy prices, labor costs, and market demand, determine the financial viability of vertical farm businesses.

Social attitudes, consumer preferences, and community engagement shape the social acceptance and integration of vertical farming into urban food systems. A coordinated and integrated approach across these sectors is essential for realizing the full potential of vertical farms to enhance urban food security and contribute to sustainable urban futures.

In conclusion, assessing the current state of the vertical farms and urban [food security nexus Meaning → The Food Security Nexus signifies the interconnectedness of food systems with broader global challenges, including climate change, water scarcity, energy security, biodiversity loss, and socio-economic stability.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-security-nexus/) reveals a sector with significant potential and ongoing challenges. Technological advancements and market growth are encouraging signs, but economic viability, environmental sustainability, and social equity remain critical areas requiring focused attention and innovation. Cross-dimensional influences underscore the complexity of integrating vertical farming into [urban food systems Meaning → Urban Food Systems represent the complex nexus of activities within metropolitan areas that procure, process, distribute, consume, and manage food and its associated waste streams.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/urban-food-systems/) and highlight the need for holistic and context-specific approaches. The future trajectory of vertical farming will depend on addressing these multifaceted challenges and leveraging its inherent advantages to create resilient, sustainable, and equitable urban food systems.

![Image 8: A sustainable future vision presents a textured concrete ground juxtaposed with a high tech industrial panel, emblematic of innovation and design focused on smart infrastructure. Circularity principles in this system highlights commitment to reducing carbon footprints in urban development and ensures resource management throughout construction. This integration blends durability with sustainable construction emphasizing waste reduction through smart material use.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-future-vision-ground-level-industrial-minimalism-high-tech.jpg)

Atrophy
-------

A negative future scenario for the vertical farms and urban food security nexus, termed “Atrophy,” paints a picture of unrealized potential and exacerbation of existing vulnerabilities. In this scenario, the initial promise of vertical farming to revolutionize urban food systems falters, leading to stagnation, limited impact, and potentially even negative consequences for food security and sustainability. This trajectory is characterized by a confluence of technological, economic, social, and environmental setbacks, hindering the widespread adoption and beneficial integration of vertical farms into urban landscapes.

> In a negative scenario, vertical farms fail to scale and address urban food security due to economic, technological, and social challenges, exacerbating existing food system vulnerabilities.

![Image 9: A minimalist spherical design embodying future technology innovations offers a glimpse into the commitment to circular economy and sustainability in the design paradigm. The sleek surface and central dark-lensed circular inset hint at futuristic advancements in data processing and bio-integration focused on ecological balance. The subdued tones and sharp contrasts convey efficiency, and sustainability principles shaping technology's evolution toward sustainable development goals and ecological preservation, signifying eco-friendly product lifecycles.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sleek-spherical-design-reflecting-a-sustainable-future-innovation-in-circular.jpg)
### Technological Stagnation and Inefficiency

In the “Atrophy” scenario, technological advancements in vertical farming slow down or fail to deliver anticipated breakthroughs in efficiency and cost reduction. **LED lighting technology** plateaus in terms of energy efficiency gains, and the cost of advanced lighting systems remains prohibitively high for widespread adoption. Environmental control systems, while sophisticated, prove to be overly complex and prone to malfunctions, leading to operational inefficiencies and increased maintenance costs. Automation efforts fail to achieve significant labor cost reductions or improve operational reliability, with robots and automated systems proving to be less adaptable and robust than anticipated.

Data analytics and AI applications remain underutilized or fail to deliver meaningful insights for optimizing growing protocols, resulting in suboptimal yields and resource utilization. This technological stagnation prevents vertical farms from achieving the necessary efficiency gains to become economically competitive and environmentally sustainable at scale.

![Image 10: The architectural study showcases elements of sustainability including sleek reflective glass which might represent green infrastructure and its futuristic design elements. The muted palette suggests a focus on resource efficient materials promoting climate resilience. Intersecting beams indicate a conscious eco design in support of green technology and innovation.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/architectural-transparency-a-window-into-sustainable-building-solutions-and-eco.jpg)
### Economic Unviability and Market Failure

Economically, the “Atrophy” scenario is marked by widespread unviability and market failure of vertical farming ventures. High capital costs and operational expenses continue to plague the sector, making it difficult for vertical farms to achieve profitability, especially in competitive food markets. **Energy costs** remain volatile and high, further eroding profit margins and making vertical farm produce unaffordable for mainstream consumers. Lack of access to financing and investment capital stifles innovation and expansion, limiting the scalability of successful vertical farming models.

Market competition from conventional agriculture and other urban farming initiatives intensifies, further squeezing vertical farm businesses. Consumer demand for vertical farm produce remains niche and fails to reach the mass market, as consumers are either unwilling to pay premium prices or unconvinced of the added value proposition. This economic stagnation leads to closures of vertical farms, investor disillusionment, and a contraction of the sector.

--- SOURCE 2: Applications of vertical farming in urban agriculture ---
URL: https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020

SUMMARY:
Applying vertical farming technologies globally may provide new solutions to address global food security, alleviate urban land resource constraints, and promote sustainable agricultural development (De Bon et al., 2010). Through pollution-free growing environments, a stable year-round supply of products, traceability system assurance, reduced food transportation distances, flexibility, scalability, and integration with other technologies, vertical farming can provide urban residents with safe, fresh, and diverse agricultural products, promote sustainable agricultural development, and improve overall quality of life. The Institute of Urban Agriculture of the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (IUA-CAAS) proposed a concept for a skyscraper crop factory that would integrate vertical farming with technologies, such as the Internet of Things, energy conservation, new energy utilization, resource circulation, and sustainable development (Zhang et al., 2023).

Summary
-------

With the acceleration of global urbanization, vertical farming and urban agriculture have been proposed for addressing food security and sustainable development challenges. As a modern agricultural technology, vertical farming plays an increasingly important role in urban agriculture because of its efficient use of space, resources, and energy. Vertical farming maximizes land use by cultivating crops in stacked layers, allowing large-scale agricultural production in a limited space, thus alleviating land resource shortages. By precisely controlling environmental factors, such as light, temperature, humidity, CO 2, and nutrient solutions, vertical farming provides crops with optimal growing conditions and increases yields while reducing reliance on fertilizers and pesticides and minimizing negative environmental impacts. Vertical farming has become an essential component of urban agriculture for food production. Owing to the efforts of researchers in related fields, vertical farming technology has made considerable progress in China. Optimizing the design and operation of vertical farms enables them to adapt better to urban environments and become sustainable pillars of the urban food supply chains. Despite progress, more research is needed to further improve vertical farming technology to meet the increasing demand for food in growing urban populations and achieve sustainable agricultural development. This article discusses the applications, technological advancements, and prospects of vertical farming for urban agriculture.

Introduction
------------

### Significance of this study

_What is already known on this subject?_

•

Plant factories, also known as vertical farms, have been recognized for their potential to revolutionize agriculture in urban settings. The concept involves growing plants indoors in controlled environments using artificial lighting, temperature regulation, and hydroponic or aeroponic systems.

_What are the new findings?_

•

New findings in this field continually emerge, focusing on refining techniques to optimize plant growth, energy efficiency, and resource utilization. Research is also exploring the integration of automation and artificial intelligence to enhance productivity and reduce labor costs.

_What is the expected impact on horticulture?_

•

Vertical farming can potentially increase food security in urban areas by providing locally grown fresh produce, reduce transportation costs and carbon emissions associated with food distribution, and offer a sustainable solution to global food demand challenges. Additionally, the controlled environment allows for the cultivation of high-value crops and medicinal plants, expanding the scope of horticultural practices.

Vertical farming is a production model that uses indoor environmental control technology to grow crops in a closed environment ([Kozai et al., 2019](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr34); [Ji et al., 2023](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr28)). It usually adopts a stacked planting architecture, utilizes artificial light sources ([Kaiser et al., 2024](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr29)), and tightly controls temperature, humidity, CO 2 concentration, nutrient solution flow ([Baiyin et al., 2024](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr10)), and other parameters to improve crop yield and quality ([Shamshiri et al., 2018](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr43)). With the acceleration of global urbanization and increasing scarcity of agricultural resources, traditional agricultural models face many challenges, such as limited land, scarce water resources, and climate change ([Maja and Ayano, 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr37)). Vertical farming has high development potential and far-reaching impact on the global agricultural industry ([Giller et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr17)). In the current context of global land resource scarcity, accelerated urbanization, and climate change, vertical farming is drawing increasing attention and investment as a sustainable and efficient agricultural model ([Van Delden et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr49)).

Vertical farming is widely applied in urban environments and has demonstrated advantages in addressing challenges faced by traditional agriculture ([Specht et al., 2014](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr46)). First, vertical farming offers a solution for growing food in limited space ([Kalantari et al., 2018](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr30)). By utilizing multilayered planting structures, vertical farming transforms land surfaces into stacked growing spaces, thereby increasing land-utilization efficiency ([Al-Kodmany, 2018](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr02)). Second, vertical farming used advanced technology and intelligent systems for environmental control to regulate factors, such as light, temperature, humidity, and CO 2 concentration, and provides optimal conditions for plant growth. This precise environmental control helps increase crop yield and quality and reduces the demand for fertilizers and pesticides, thus lowering the negative impact of crop cultivation on the environment. Thus, vertical farming can achieve higher yields than traditional farmland exploitation, providing city residents with more fresh agricultural products.

Consumers are increasingly in pursuit of organic, pollution-free, and sustainable food, and vertical farming can better meet these demands by providing healthy and safe food ([Avgoustaki and Xydis, 2020a](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr06)). By building vertical farming in urban areas, agricultural products can be grown closer to consumers, providing fresher and safer agricultural products and ensuring the safety and quality of food.

From an international perspective, Japan has conducted a considerable amount of research into vertical farming ([Al-Kodmany, 2018](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr02)). The large-scale application of vertical farming for growing vegetables in Japan has become an international model, with many countries following suit ([Ampim et al., 2022](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr03)). Other developed countries, such as the United States ([Van Gerrewey et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr50)), the Netherlands ([Blom et al., 2022](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr11)), and Singapore ([Jaeger et al., 2023](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr27)), are actively promoting and applying vertical farming technologies. In China, several major cities, such as Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Shenzhen, and Chengdu, have successfully built vertical farms to meet the demands of urban residents for high-quality agricultural products ([Xu et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr53)).

Vertical farming represents a modern form of agriculture that is expected to play an increasingly important role in food production ([Hati and Singh, 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr22)) given its efficient land utilization, sustainable production methods, and alignment with consumer demands. Applying vertical farming technologies globally may provide new solutions to address global food security, alleviate urban land resource constraints, and promote sustainable agricultural development ([De Bon et al., 2010](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr15)).

Applications of vertical farming in urban agriculture
-----------------------------------------------------

### Food safety assurance

As an advanced indoor agricultural system, vertical farming has extensive applications in urban agriculture and plays a crucial role in ensuring food safety ([Gómez et al., 2019](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr18)). Vertical farming adopts enclosed growing environments in which temperature, humidity, light, CO 2, and nutrient solutions ([Baiyin et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr09)) are tightly controlled. These conditions minimize the impact of external environmental pollution on agricultural products and thus enable the growth of pesticide-free, safe, and healthy agricultural products. Vertical farms are not affected by seasons and weather, providing a stable supply of agricultural products throughout the year, ensuring that urban residents always have access to fresh and high-quality agricultural products, reducing their dependence on imported food and improving food supply security. The use of intelligent management systems to achieve real-time monitoring, data recording, and traceability of agricultural products enhances consumer trust in product quality and safety. In addition, vertical farming efficiently utilizes land resources, saves water and energy, reduces the use of chemical pesticides and fertilizers, decreases the risk of environmental pollution, and promotes food safety.

Vertical farms are often located within or near urban areas, which reduces food transportation distance, allows agricultural products to reach consumers more quickly, lowers the risk of food contamination during transportation, and maintains food freshness and safety ([Armanda et al., 2019](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr05)). Owing to the high degree of automation in vertical farming, production efficiency and quality stability have improved, further ensuring food safety.

The flexibility and scalability built into the systems allows for adjustments and increased production capacity according to market demand ([Avgoustaki and Xydis, 2020b](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr07)). Through precise cultivation management and data analysis, vertical farms can efficiently utilize resources, minimize waste, and reduce environmental pollution. Furthermore, vertical farming can diversify agricultural production by cultivating vegetables and fruits, as well as various herbal plants and flowers, to meet the demand for different agricultural products.

To achieve efficient production management and enable decision support, vertical farming systems can be integrated with other technologies, such as the Internet of Things, artificial intelligence (AI), and big data analysis ([Chang et al., 2022](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr14)). Through real-time monitoring and data analysis, problems during cultivation can be promptly detected and solved, thereby improving production efficiency and product quality. Moreover, AI technology can be used for accurate predictions and optimization to maximize the yield and quality of agricultural products.

In summary, as an advanced indoor agricultural system, vertical farming has immense potential for use in urban agriculture. Through pollution-free growing environments, a stable year-round supply of products, traceability system assurance, reduced food transportation distances, flexibility, scalability, and integration with other technologies, vertical farming can provide urban residents with safe, fresh, and diverse agricultural products, promote sustainable agricultural development, and improve overall quality of life. With further technological advancements, vertical farming will continue to play an increasingly important role in ensuring food safety in urban agriculture.

### Environmental protection

From the perspective of sustainability and carbon neutrality, vertical farming not only improves the output efficiency of urban agriculture but also effectively reduces the impact on the environment and natural resources, making urban agriculture more sustainable. Vertical farms utilize advanced irrigation systems to recycle water resources and reduce waste. Compared with traditional agricultural methods, they can save more than 70% of water, alleviating water shortages in urban areas ([Wang et al., 2002](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr51)). Closed production spaces can effectively prevent the occurrence of crop diseases and pests as well as decreasing the extent of environmental pollution and eliminating potential risks to human health from chemical pesticides. This ensures the quality and safety of crops while meeting modern society's demand for a healthy and sustainable environment ([Koohafkan et al., 2012](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr32)).

Regarding energy usage, vertical farms use more electricity energy than fossil energy. The use of LED lights not only provides stable lighting conditions and uniform light intensity but also relieves energy consumption pressure. Compared with the energy demands of traditional field planting methods (tractors and other agricultural machinery require the consumption of fossil fuels), vertical farms are more energy efficient, which can help alleviate energy shortage issues faced by urban areas. Furthermore, the natural circulation system formed by vertical farming maximizes the recycling of organic waste, effectively reduces the cost of organic waste disposal and its impact on the atmosphere, and helps the transition to carbon neutrality ([Kozai and Niu, 2020](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr33)).

Vertical farming improves land-use efficiency ([Van Gerrewey et al., 2021](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr50)) by maximizing planting space and arable area. This helps to achieve maximal use of land resources and effectively increases the yield of urban agriculture.

In summary, vertical farming has important application value and practical significance in urban agriculture. Leveraging the various advantages of vertical farming to promote the development of sustainable, healthy, efficient, and sustainable urban agriculture is one of the important paths for future urban sustainable development.

### Technological advances in vertical farming in the context of urban agriculture

Vertical farming represents a highly technology-intensive industry, with relatively high initial investment costs and operating energy consumption levels ([Petrovics and Giezen, 2022](https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/10.17660/eJHS.2024/020#core-collateral-bibr41)). Countries worldwide have been investing in research to reduce energy consumption and enhance product quality in response to industry demands. Progress has been made, especially in areas such as plant light formulas, LED light source development, light–temperature coupled energy-saving environmental control, vegetable nutrition quality improvement, and AI-based management systems. Vertical Farming as a Solution for Urban Food Deserts
=====================================================

Vertical Farming: A Sustainable Solution for Urban Food Deserts
---------------------------------------------------------------

### Introduction

In today’s rapidly urbanizing world, access to fresh and nutritious food has become a pressing concern, particularly in densely populated cities. This article aims to explore the concept of vertical farming and its relevance in addressing urban food deserts. By finding innovative solutions to cultivate food in limited spaces, vertical farming has the potential to revolutionize urban agriculture and ensure food security for all.

Historical Background
---------------------

Vertical farming has a rich history, with key milestones and developments shaping its evolution. The concept can be traced back to the Hanging Gardens of Babylon and has continuously evolved to meet the growing demands of urban populations. However, the emergence of urban food deserts, areas with limited access to affordable and nutritious food, has created an urgent need for alternative farming methods.

Key Concepts and Definitions
----------------------------

Vertical farming is a system of cultivation that utilizes vertically stacked layers to grow crops, often in controlled environments. It differs from traditional agriculture methods by maximizing space utilization through the use of hydroponics, aquaponics, and aeroponics. Urban food deserts, on the other hand, refer to areas where fresh and healthy food is scarce, often due to socioeconomic factors and limited access to grocery stores.

![Image 6: Vertical Farming as a Solution for Urban Food Deserts](https://green.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/R-116-1024x680.jpg)

Main Discussion Points
----------------------

### Benefits of Vertical Farming in Urban Food Deserts

Increased food production in limited space: Vertical farming allows for the cultivation of crops in vertical layers, maximizing land utilization and increasing food production per square foot.

Reduced transportation costs and carbon emissions: By growing food directly in urban areas, vertical farming minimizes the need for long-distance transportation, reducing carbon emissions associated with food miles.

Year-round cultivation and climate control: Vertical farms can provide a consistent supply of fresh produce regardless of seasonal changes, ensuring a reliable food source for urban communities.

### Social and Economic Impacts of Vertical Farming in Urban Food Deserts

Improved access to fresh and healthy food: Vertical farming brings nutritious food closer to underserved communities, addressing the issue of food deserts and promoting better health outcomes.

Job creation and economic stimulation: The establishment of vertical farms creates employment opportunities, stimulating economic growth in urban areas while fostering community development.

Community empowerment and self-sufficiency: Vertical farming can empower communities to take control of their food production, reducing reliance on external food sources and promoting self-sufficiency.

### Environmental Sustainability of Vertical Farming in Urban Food Deserts

Efficient use of water and energy: Vertical farms employ advanced irrigation systems and LED lighting, minimizing water usage and energy consumption compared to traditional agriculture.

![Image 7: Vertical Farming as a Solution for Urban Food Deserts](https://green.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/0296fd_0871c346a8a648cc9b31e004ed198973mv2.webp)

Reduction of chemical inputs and waste: With precise nutrient delivery and pest control, vertical farming significantly reduces the need for pesticides and herbicides, resulting in reduced chemical pollution and waste.

Preservation of agricultural land and biodiversity: By utilizing vertical space within urban areas, vertical farming helps preserve valuable agricultural land outside cities, promoting biodiversity conservation.

Case Studies or Examples
------------------------

Several successful vertical farming projects have emerged in urban food desert areas, demonstrating the potential of this innovative approach. For instance, Gotham Greens, a leading urban agricultural company, operates commercial-scale vertical farms across several cities, providing fresh produce to local communities. These case studies highlight the challenges faced, such as initial investment costs and technological complexities, but also showcase the benefits achieved.

Current Trends or Developments
------------------------------

Advancements in vertical farming technologies and techniques continue to shape the future of this industry. Innovations such as vertical aeroponics and modular farming systems have increased efficiency and scalability. Furthermore, the integration of vertical farming with renewable energy sources like solar power presents opportunities for sustainable and self-sufficient urban food production. Ongoing research also explores the nutritional quality and safety of vertical farm produce, ensuring its suitability for human consumption.

Challenges or Controversies
---------------------------

While vertical farming holds great promise, it is not without challenges and controversies. Concerns have been raised regarding the high initial costs and energy requirements of vertical farming systems. Additionally, scalability remains a potential limitation, as implementing large-scale vertical farms may pose logistical and economic challenges. Differing viewpoints exist regarding the effectiveness and feasibility of vertical farming as a comprehensive solution for addressing urban food deserts.

![Image 8: Vertical Farming as a Solution for Urban Food Deserts](https://green.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/08d113dccf7bfd294cd8e4472a124c12-1024x1024.jpg)

Future Outlook
--------------

Looking ahead, vertical farming is poised to play an increasingly significant role in addressing urban food deserts. Continued advancements and innovations are expected to enhance the sustainability and efficiency of vertical farming systems. As technology improves and costs decrease, vertical farming has the potential to become a mainstream method of food production, contributing to improved food security, public health, and environmental sustainability.

Conclusion
----------

Vertical farming offers a sustainable and practical solution to combat urban food deserts. By increasing food production in limited spaces, improving access to fresh and healthy food, and reducing environmental impacts, vertical farming holds immense potential. As we move towards a more urbanized future, it is crucial to embrace innovative solutions like vertical farming to ensure food security for all and create healthier, more resilient communities.

Research

ABSTRACT
--------

Food systems are vulnerable to socioeconomic and environmental changes, and increasing food system resilience has emerged as a key public policy priority. Vertical agriculture is one potential strategy to contribute to food system resilience. To date, the literature exploring this technology has focused on consumer perceptions, with less examination of broader social-ecological system considerations or on-the-ground implications for vulnerable food system actors. This study explores local food system stakeholders’ account of vertical agriculture’s potential contributions to food system resilience, and specifically to food system decentralization, increasing stakeholder decision-making autonomy and diversifying food systems. The results of an exploratory focus group with 16 participants, including local government officials and members of community food organizations, are presented. Participants discussed how vertical agriculture can contribute to food system decentralization by operating across a variety of scales and in public institutions, although local land-use zoning barriers, competing land use priorities, and lack of community acceptance may hinder its widespread implementation. Further, vertical agriculture may contribute to food system diversification by increasing the amount of regionally available produce in the face of future supply disruptions. The potential implications of vertical agriculture for increasing decision-making autonomy will vary, depending on whether it is governed at more or less accessible scales and forms. It is recommended that further research consider diverse scenarios and engage with wider stakeholder and community groups for vertical agriculture’s planning and implementation, particularly in less explored land use contexts, such as in agricultural regions or in public institutions.

INTRODUCTION
------------

It is increasingly recognized that our food systems are vulnerable to social, economic, and environmental change (FAO et al. 2023). A complex variety of factors make food systems more or less vulnerable to disturbances or shocks (Ericksen 2008). At a local or regional level, individuals having few options or strategies to access food and there being a low diversity of regionally grown crops have historically increased food system vulnerability (Fraser 2007). At a global level, the food system is ever more tightly interconnected through water, energy, transportation, and political systems, thereby increasing the potential for “synchronous failure” where the impacts of regional shocks (e.g., severe weather events) propagate over time and space (Homer-Dixon et al. 2015). Other factors, such as mounting global oligopoly of food producers, distributors, and retailers, have similarly exacerbated the vulnerability of the food system (Clapp 2023). Intensive monoculture practices and concomitant declines in agricultural biodiversity have increased the susceptibility of crops to pests, weeds, and disease (Altieri et al. 2015). Major events, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and war in Ukraine, have highlighted these dynamics in stark ways, where cross-border closures and supply disruptions have made food access and distribution challenging, exacerbating the risk of hunger worldwide (Carey et al. 2021). Such concerns and the comprehensive effects of interacting food systems have spurred a large body of literature exploring ways to increase food system resilience. Developing resilience in food systems can therefore be a lever of change for addressing food insecurity and vulnerability (Townsend et al. 2021). Resilience is a measure of a system’s ability to adapt and transform in response to disturbance (Walker et al. 2004).

One such effort to build food system resilience is vertical agriculture. Vertical agriculture refers to a suite of tools and technologies for growing food “upward,” not “outward,” thereby sparing land for conservation (Despommier 2010). Often (but not exclusively) using hydroponic, aeroponic, or aquaponic nutrient circulation systems alongside stacked grow-beds, vertical agriculture is highly water-, nutrient-, and land-efficient (Kozai et al. 2019, Halgamuge et al. 2021). Vertical agriculture promises, from a food system resilience standpoint, to safeguard food production from the impacts of climate change, such as adverse weather or pests and disease (Henry 2020). Similarly, it promises to contribute to developing more decentralized food systems, growing fresh vegetables locally and year-round. As currently practiced, however, vertical agriculture is relatively niche (in scale), not widespread, and limited to a few crops of moderate nutritional value (Goodman and Minner 2019).

The social scientific literature exploring vertical agriculture has only recently emerged. According to one recent systematic literature review, the socioeconomic literature regarding controlled environment agriculture comprises less than 10% of the current body of research, leading authors to argue that “socio-economic research...still lags behind biological or technical research” (Dsouza et al. 2023:8). Our research contributes to addressing this gap. Further, social scientific literature has largely focused on consumer perceptions of vertical agriculture, and specifically if consumers are willing to buy or try vertical agriculture products. Research has found positive perceptions of vertical agriculture’s contribution to fresh local food availability and utilization of vacant or small spaces, as well as skepticism regarding its “naturalness,” health aspects, nutrition, and impact on conventional food systems (Specht et al. 2019, Broad et al. 2022).

To date, literature has yet to explore civil society perspectives for vertical agriculture’s potential contribution to food system resilience, specifically in the North American context. In particular, few studies (with some exceptions: see Carolan 2020), have explored vertical agriculture from a planning perspective, asking where vertical agriculture fits in local and regional food systems. Doing so can elucidate key gaps and concerns that civil society stakeholders have as vertical agriculture is being considered as a potentially feasible strategy to build food system resilience in cities or regions. It is crucial to gather public views of vertical agriculture and identify existential and perceived barriers or potential hindrances to achieving its most widespread benefits. In particular, it is important to understand the perspectives of members of civil society who are actively involved in governing and shaping local food systems, such as local government, food system organizations, farmers, and retailers. Doing so can help to identify tensions and synergies in terms of new food production technology implementation and their potential implications for food system resilience. We note here and in the conclusion that vertical agriculture is but one strategy to bolster local food systems and, like all emerging technologies for food production, has tradeoffs (see Glaros et al. 2022). Thus, we suggest that vertical agriculture should be considered among a variety of strategies and approaches to increase food system resilience (Newman et al. 2023).

This study is the first stage of a broader research portfolio, working with consumers, local government, industry, and planners to develop scenarios for the implementation of vertical agriculture in British Columbia. In this initial research, we sought to understand and unpack the prospects of vertical agriculture’s contribution to food system resilience. We focus on exploring various civil society stakeholder accounts from the Lower Mainland of British Columbia, Canada, as it is a highly agricultural region of Canada that continues to experience severe impacts of climate change. We begin by defining food system resilience and how we used it as a theoretical framework to analyze our data. We shift to highlighting key opportunities and barriers that these stakeholders emphasized for vertical agriculture’s contribution to food system resilience, including deductively examined data as well as themes that emerged from focus group discussions.

THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK
---------------------

Multiple definitions for and interpretations of resilience exist in the literature, and the term itself has evolved over the past several decades. Earlier definitions of resilience refer to a system’s ability to resist disturbance: to stay the same (Pimm 1984). Literature from the discipline of ecology instead defined resilience as a system’s capacity to maintain its function over time (Gunderson and Holling 2001). Notions of adaptation and transformation were subsequently introduced, recognizing the existence of system tipping points and the potential for reorganizing and designing new systems altogether (Walker et al. 2004). Resilience has also been applied to examine social systems, wherein it has been defined as the ability of a community or group to cope with external stresses (Adger 2000).

Resilience is often explored within the context of food systems, being defined as the “capacity over time of a food system and its units at multiple levels, to provide sufficient, appropriate and accessible food to all, in the face of various and even unforeseen disturbances” (Tendall et al. 2015:18). However, specific metrics for or definitions of food system resilience are ill defined, particularly regarding the following questions: resilience of what, to what? Resilience for whom? Resilience for how long? (Cretney 2014, Meerow and Newell 2019, Zurek et al. 2022). For the purpose of this study, we view a resilient food system as one that maintains the systems’ wide preconditions so as to ensure food security in response to social, economic, and environmental uncertainties, similar to Hodbod and Eakin (2015).

Several factors reduce food system resilience, including lack of on-farm diversity, exposure and vulnerability to disturbances through hyper-connectivity, and lack of farmer decision-making autonomy (Rotz and Fraser 2015). Studies also define targeted areas for building food system resilience and link sustainability dimensions directly to resilience considerations, such as those that recognize that social factors affect a person’s or community’s capacity to respond to food insecurity (Kaiser 2019). Other examples include research that indicates intensive monocultures provide little ecological value (Onaindia et al. 2013) while being extremely vulnerable to pests and disease, and literature that highlights how food import-dependency can result in economic vulnerability (Schipanski et al. 2015).

Recognizing these conceptual ambiguities, we distilled from the literature a few key principles for increasing food system resilience and used these to deductively shape our thematic analysis of focus group data (Hendrickson 2015, Rotz and Fraser 2015, van Wassanaer et al. 2021). These three principles are as follows:

1.   Decentralizing food production operations;
2.   Increasing the agency and capacity of actors throughout the supply chain to exercise choice, particularly for those most vulnerable (e.g., low-income groups, temporary agricultural workers); and
3.   Increasing diversity within the food system, especially across the following three areas: number of crop species grown, places in which crops are grown, and places where people can access food.

Below, we describe each of these principles in further detail, including literature relevant to food systems and food system resilience. We acknowledge that definitions of resilience are contested, especially as related to how to measure, indicate, or otherwise increase/decrease resilience. However, our purpose in this paper is not so much to define resilience per se as to better understand if and how vertical agriculture can contribute to this normative goal. Although others may or may not agree with our characterization of resilience, we nevertheless consider the following principles as key components of a potentially more comprehensive definition. We recognize that these principles are likely to have inherent tradeoffs and are not ubiquitous in all vertical farming contexts. Our case study, however, illustrates how we captured some of the critical aspects of resilience relevant to vertical agriculture in the Fraser Valley region of British Columbia. As a result, the principles guided our exploration and evaluation of vertical agriculture benefits as well as adoption barriers in a more practical manner.

### Decentralization

Food systems have become increasingly concentrated and consolidated within the private sector (Clapp 2023). Concentration of food systems increases their opacity, where it becomes difficult to trace commodity flows, actor networks, or other important information across the global value chain (Clapp 2014). This has implications for consumer trust (knowing where and how food was produced), responding to disease and pest outbreaks, or assigning responsibility to malpractice and “rule breaking” as it occurs (Tian 2016, Iost Filho et al. 2019). Highly centralized, consolidated food systems are vulnerable because they have decreased capacity to adapt or transform in the face of disturbance (Hendrickson 2015). Decreasing concentration is an important food system development strategy because it helps to increase supply chain transparency and prevent widespread disruptions resulting from synchronous failure between tightly interconnected systems (Homer-Dixon et al. 2015). Decentralizing food systems would involve increasing the number of food system actors, from production through consumption and waste, operating at small and medium scales for local/regional distribution.

### Agency and choice

Where actors within food systems have fewer opportunities to make decisions and choices (i.e., exercise agency) in response to changes or disturbances, a system can be said to lack resilience. This is acute for small and medium scales of farmers, where external factors, such as sectoral concentration (Rotz and Fraser 2015) and climate change (Higgins et al. 2017), can disincentivize the adoption of new tools, crops, technologies, production practices, or regulatory requirements. Farmers having the ability to respond to disturbance, both as an adaptation and/or as a way of transformation in the face of disturbance, is crucial from a food system resilience lens. These challenges are also acute for consumers, in particular for vulnerable members of the public who may lack food security or the knowledge and resources for accessing food in the wake of adversities. Having diverse options to procure food (from the market, shared between neighbors, grown at home, etc.) is important to maintain individual food security (Sen 1983, Fraser 2007). Agency and choice are also imperative for small-scale producers specializing in local distribution. These producers often are in tune with consumer preferences but are at a competitive disadvantage compared to large-scale businesses (producers-retailers-distributors) better positioned (by virtue of economies of scale) to influence food policy decisions. When a small number of large-scale producers have the most power to affect change in the food system, opportunities for small and medium-scale producers to advocate for themselves and respond to disturbance is reduced (Rotz and Fraser 2015).

### Diversity

Systems can be described as more resilient if they have a high level of diversity and what some term functional redundancy: a concept from ecology for when more than one component of a system performs a similar role. As one example, if there are many producers growing diverse crops, a food system might be considered to have high functional redundancy, compared to one where a single producer is growing one or two crops. Food systems are more susceptible to pests, disease, and other disturbances that can easily transmit throughout a system when there is less crop and genetic diversity (Rotz and Fraser 2015). Diversity also translates to socioeconomic factors, including the function of food systems. For example, Hodbod and Eakin (2015) find that singular prioritization of a food system function (e.g., profit maximization) can precipitate vulnerabilities. Hertel et al. (2021) state that any attempt to integrate diversification of food production will need to transcend soil-based farming and encompass other subsectors, such as vertical and urban farming.

METHODS
-------

This research was conducted in collaboration with three industry partners: QuantoTech Solutions, i-Open Technologies, and Direct Food Store. QuantoTech Solutions develops and operates vertical farms; i-Open Technologies Group designed and developed _Agrilyze_, a map-based platform for storing, displaying, and analyzing food systems-related data; and Direct Food Store is an e-commerce and food distribution platform. The collaboration with industry partners was based on efforts to model and illustrate the potential benefits and drawbacks of developing the vertical agriculture industry in the Fraser Valley region, British Columbia (Fig. 1). This region hosts a strong and diverse agricultural sector, comprising a significant portion of the overall agricultural activity (39% of gross farm receipts in 2015) in British Columbia (Fraser Valley Regional District 2017). Further, the region was subject to major flooding events in November 2021, making this region an important case study in the context of integrated planning for resilience to climate change (Newell et al. 2022).

### Focus groups

Focus groups are a widely accepted and undertaken research activity to engage stakeholders and solicit their opinion on a given research topic (Nyumba et al. 2018). The protocol for this method typically involves a researcher delivering a set of semi-structured questions to a group of individuals (usually around 10), discussing the questions as a group, and qualitatively analyzing the resulting transcripts for themes. Prior to the focus group, researchers attended public virtual meetings with community food networks and organizations in the region. Members of these networks were invited to the focus group discussion, given their active and on-going practical planning, engagement, and program implementation experience related to food systems in the region of study. The researchers sent invitation letters to individuals within the community network and organization members as well as via these networks and organizations. The invitation was also sent to community members and food systems professionals/stakeholders with whom the researchers had previously engaged in other research efforts.

In total, 16 participants ultimately attended the focus group (Table 1). Research has found an optimal size for focus groups to be between seven and 10 individuals to ensure all voices have a better chance of being heard. As such, three breakout groups were created in order to undertake discussions in a more efficient and effective manner. Overall, the study participants represented a broad cross-section of community food system actors, including local governments, non-governmental organizations, industry stakeholders, and food retailers.

This focus group was exploratory and structured to gauge general themes and potential avenues for further research and study with stakeholders in the Fraser Valley region. The focus group ran for two hours in February 2022, and was conducted via Zoom and CoLabS, a web-based platform for interactive engagement and collaboration (see Jost et al. 2021 for similar use of this platform). Following full group discussion, two small groups (8 participants in each) were formed in Zoom breakout sessions. The focus group questions were categorized into three respective themes: “vertical farms and communities” (i.e., opportunities and barriers for vertical farm integration into local communities); “vertical farms and products/crops” (i.e., perspectives on vertically grown products); and “vertical farms and consumers” (i.e., about willingness to try vertical agriculture products). Questions within each theme focused on the potential contribution of vertical agriculture to food system resilience and sustainability. The first few minutes of each session began with participants posting comments related to the questions of the theme in CoLabS. Researchers then facilitated discussion using these comments.

### Analysis

To deductively examine our qualitative data we adopted three following principles of food system resilience: how vertical agriculture development may decentralize food systems, increase individual agency and capacity to respond to disturbances, and diversify food systems. A combination of deductive analysis (using the three principles for food system resilience defined above) and inductive approaches (cataloguing additional and emergent themes outside the three principles) were used to thematically code the focus group data (see Tables 2 and 3). Combining deductive with inductive approaches to data analysis is a strategy to ensure that a holistic account of all that was said during the focus group is captured (Azungah 2018).
